column4: Medical imaging that reconstructs cross-sectional body images from many X-ray measurements and computer processing.
Low-dose X-ray imaging optimized for breast cancer screening, diagnosis, and follow-up.
Diagnostic imaging that uses radioactive tracers to measure physiological function inside the body.
Functional imaging method that detects coincident gamma rays from positron-emitting tracers to map metabolism and physiology.
Automated instruments and workflows for high-throughput clinical chemistry, hematology, microbiology, and immunoassay testing.
Laboratory tests that use antibody-antigen binding to detect hormones, proteins, pathogens, and biomarkers.
Molecular diagnostic tests that amplify nucleic acids to detect pathogens, mutations, or genetic markers.
Portable diagnostic tests performed near patients for rapid clinical decisions outside centralized laboratories.
Noninvasive optical monitoring of arterial oxygen saturation and pulse using photoplethysmography.
Clinical systems that combine imaging, laboratory, records, and guidelines to assist diagnosis under auditable medical oversight.
Engineered systems that remove solids, pathogens, organic matter, and nutrients from sewage before discharge or reuse.
Biological wastewater treatment using aerated microbial flocs to break down organic pollution.
Instrument networks that measure pollutants such as particulates, ozone, nitrogen oxides, sulfur dioxide, and volatile organic compounds.
Vehicle exhaust treatment devices that use catalysts to reduce carbon monoxide, hydrocarbons, and nitrogen oxides.
Computer models that simulate atmosphere, ocean, land, ice, and energy flows to project climate behavior.
Standardized accounting systems for estimating, reporting, and verifying greenhouse gas emissions and removals.
Systems that separate carbon dioxide from industrial or power-plant streams and store it underground or in durable products.
Biodiversity and pathogen surveillance using DNA shed into water, soil, or air samples.
Sensor, forecast, communication, and response systems that warn communities before floods, storms, fires, tsunamis, or other hazards.
Engineered systems that chemically remove carbon dioxide directly from ambient air for storage or use.
Self-propelled farm vehicles that provide mobile power for plowing, hauling, planting, and later many field operations.
Manufactured nutrient inputs such as nitrogen, phosphorus, and potassium fertilizers used to raise crop yields.
Machines that combine reaping, threshing, and winnowing grain into one mechanized harvest operation.
Chemical and application technologies for controlling insects, weeds, fungi, and other agricultural pests.
Controlled plant breeding and seed multiplication systems that produce high-yielding hybrid crop varieties.
Crop varieties engineered with specific genetic traits such as pest resistance, herbicide tolerance, or nutritional changes.
Low-pressure irrigation that delivers water directly to plant roots through emitters and tubing.
Soilless crop production using nutrient solutions, controlled water chemistry, and managed root-zone environments.
Indoor or enclosed farming systems that control light, temperature, humidity, nutrients, and carbon dioxide for crop production.
Uncrewed aerial systems used for crop scouting, spraying, mapping, livestock monitoring, and field analytics.
Breeding and genomic selection programs for crops tolerant to heat, drought, flooding, salinity, pests, and shifting seasons.
